core_intuitions: Prefill is compute-bound and decode is memory-bandwidth-bound because of WEIGHT REUSE: a long prompt pushes T_p tokens through each weight matrix loaded once from HBM (fat matrix x matrix, high arithmetic intensity), while decode streams the entire weight set from HBM to advance just ONE token per sequence (skinny vector x matrix, low arithmetic intensity) — same bytes moved, vastly fewer FLOPs per byte.

These opposite resource profiles mean the phases want different hardware (fast-FLOP GPUs for prefill, high-HBM-bandwidth GPUs for decode) and actively FIGHT each other when mixed on one GPU under continuous batching.

Prefill-decode interference: one long prefill monopolizes the GPU for tens of ms, so every in-flight decode sequence is stalled and the next token arrives late — a bursty P99 inter-token-latency (ITL) spike even though average latency looks fine.

Chunked prefill bounds that stall: slice the long prompt into fixed-size chunks (C ~ 512) and interleave one chunk with a full decode step every iteration, so decode is never fully preempted and ITL inflation is capped at one chunk's compute time — at a small TTFT cost.

Disaggregation eliminates interference entirely by running prefill and decode on separate pools, paying instead a KV-cache transfer whose cost is set by the interconnect: ~1 ms on NVLink/NVSwitch but tens of ms over cross-node InfiniBand, which can rival the prefill itself.

The disaggregation lever is a resource-allocation tradeoff: split a fixed GPU budget between a prefill pool and a decode pool, and the optimal split depends on the workload's prompt-to-output ratio (long-prompt RAG needs more prefill; long-output chat needs more decode).

existing.name: disagg-pd-architecture
existing.issue: The vertical dataflow (Router -> Prefill Pool -> KV transfer -> Decode Pool -> Client) is clear and correct in both themes, and the amber KV-transfer arrow is well emphasized. Two small blemishes: the 'COMPUTE-BOUND' / 'BANDWIDTH-BOUND' badges sit flush against (and their text slightly rides over) the right edge of the pool boxes; and in the STATIC first frame an animated 'KV ~1 GB' pill is captured half off-canvas as a clipped stray element at the very top-left corner, which reads as a rendering bug to anyone who does not press play.
existing.improvement: Nudge both badges ~12-16px left so the full word sits comfortably inside the pool rectangle with margin. Make every animated element (the 'request' pill, the 'KV ~1 GB' pill, the token circles) start at opacity 0 / off-path at t=0 so the static first frame shows none of them clipped at the canvas edge — the figure must be fully clean as a still. No structural change; the architecture and lifecycle legend are otherwise excellent.

existing.name: chunked-prefill-iterations
existing.issue: Renders clearly in both light and dark. Successfully conveys that each engine iteration = one fixed-width prefill chunk (blue) + one decode token per persistent sequence (green), that the same 3 sequences are never preempted, and that C bounds the per-iteration stall; the top progress bar and faded third iteration communicate 'repeats until the prompt is consumed.' This is exactly the SOLUTION-side intuition the chapter needs and needs no change.

new_figures.name: prefill-decode-reuse-contrast
new_figures.concept: WHY prefill is compute-bound and decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, framed as weight reuse: the same weight bytes are streamed from HBM in both phases, but prefill amortizes that read across T_p prompt tokens (matrix x matrix, high intensity) while decode amortizes it across a single token per sequence (vector x matrix, low intensity).
new_figures.rationale: This is the physical bedrock of the entire chapter — every downstream idea (why the phases interfere, why disaggregation uses heterogeneous hardware, why decode wants bandwidth and prefill wants FLOPs) rests on it. The chapter ASSERTS the distinction with equations and a 'key insight' sentence but never SHOWS it; the existing architecture figure only labels the pools 'COMPUTE-BOUND'/'BANDWIDTH-BOUND' as a conclusion. A reader who sees the reuse contrast can predict the whole chapter's logic. It is the single most load-bearing missing intuition here.
new_figures.placement: ### Decode: Memory-Bandwidth-Bound Vector-Matrix Multiplications — anchor immediately after the 'The key insight:' paragraph that ends '...more, bandwidth-rich GPUs for decode.'
new_figures.brief:
A two-panel before/after contrast that makes 'same bytes, different FLOPs-per-byte' visceral. Both panels share ONE identical weight block drawn as a tall stack of matrix tiles labelled 'model weights (streamed from HBM every step)', and an identical wide arrow from an 'HBM' reservoir feeding those weights — emphasize with a faint tie-line or a repeated 'same bytes moved' tag that the byte cost of the weight read is IDENTICAL in both panels. The ONLY thing that differs is how many token-rows flow through.

LEFT panel 'PREFILL (process the whole prompt at once)': draw a FAT activation block of many stacked rows (label 'T_p prompt tokens', show ~6-8 rows to imply many) flowing through the weight stack, producing many outputs. Annotate: 'one weight read reused across T_p tokens', 'matrix x matrix (GEMM)', 'high arithmetic intensity -> COMPUTE-BOUND'. Small badge: 'wants peak FLOP/s'.

RIGHT panel 'DECODE (one new token per sequence)': the SAME weight stack and SAME HBM arrow, but only a SINGLE thin activation row flows through (label '1 token', optionally 'x B_d sequences'), producing one output token. Annotate: 'same weights streamed, but reused for just 1 token', 'vector x matrix (GEMV)', 'low arithmetic intensity -> MEMORY-BANDWIDTH-BOUND'. Small badge: 'wants HBM bandwidth'.

Bottom strip / punchline tying them together: 'Identical weight traffic; prefill spreads it over many tokens, decode over one -> opposite bottlenecks -> the two phases want opposite hardware and interfere when mixed.' Optionally a tiny roofline inset in each panel's corner (same axes as the roofline chapter) with prefill's dot near the flat compute ceiling and decode's dot down on the bandwidth slope, connected by a small arrow — but the matrix-shape contrast must carry the figure on its own without the inset.

Constraints: theme-safe CSS vars only — use the accent (blue) coding for the compute-bound/prefill side and the good (green) coding for the bandwidth-bound/decode side to stay consistent with the existing architecture figure's COMPUTE-BOUND/BANDWIDTH-BOUND colors. Use ONLY the symbolic quantities the chapter already uses (T_p, B_d, d_model); invent NO TFLOP/s, GB/s, or ms numbers. Everything inside the viewBox, generous whitespace, obvious left-to-right reading order, fully legible as a single static frame.

new_figures.name: prefill-decode-interference-timeline
new_figures.concept: The prefill-decode interference problem: on a shared GPU, decode sequences emit tokens at a steady low-ITL cadence until a long prompt arrives — its monolithic prefill occupies the GPU for tens of ms, stalling every decode sequence and producing a single giant gap (a P99 ITL spike) before tokens resume.
new_figures.rationale: This is the MOTIVATING PROBLEM the whole chapter exists to solve, yet nothing visualizes it — the chunked-prefill figure and the architecture figure both show SOLUTIONS. A reader needs to feel the 'before': why does a long prompt wreck P99 ITL when the average looks fine? A gantt-style timeline showing steady decode ticks shattered by one long prefill block makes the bursty-tail intuition immediate and directly sets up BOTH remedies (chunking bounds the gap; disaggregation removes it). Foundational as the problem statement.
new_figures.placement: ### The Interference Problem — anchor after the numbered paragraph 2 that ends '...the effective batch size for decode shrinks.'
new_figures.brief:
A single horizontal engine-iteration timeline (x-axis = time / engine iterations, arrowed) for ONE shared GPU, showing three or four decode sequences as lanes plus the intruding prefill.

Left two-thirds: a steady run of narrow decode iterations — draw evenly spaced small green decode ticks/blocks, and above them mark the inter-token gaps for one representative sequence with short equal double-headed arrows labelled 'ITL (small, steady)'. This is the healthy cadence.

Middle: a NEW long prompt arrives (a small 'long prompt arrives, 8K tokens' flag/arrow dropping onto the timeline). It triggers ONE wide blue monolithic 'PREFILL (whole prompt, one forward pass)' block that spans many time units — visibly much wider than any decode tick. Across the full width of that block, shade the decode lanes as STALLED (greyed / hatched, label 'all decode sequences blocked'). No green decode tokens are emitted during this span.

Right after the prefill block: decode resumes (green ticks return). Draw ONE big double-headed arrow spanning the whole stall from the last pre-prefill token to the first post-prefill token, boldly labelled 'ITL SPIKE = full prefill time -> P99 violation'. Visually contrast its length against the small steady ITL arrows on the left (maybe a faint 'vs' or size comparison).

Bottom caption strip: 'Average latency hides this — but the tail (P99 ITL) is set by the worst stall. Chunked prefill shrinks this gap to one chunk; disaggregation removes it entirely.' — explicitly pointing forward to {{fig:chunked-prefill-iterations}} and {{fig:disagg-pd-architecture}}.

Constraints: theme-safe CSS vars — blue/accent for the prefill block (consistent with the other figures), green/good for decode ticks, a warn/amber accent only for the ITL-spike callout arrow. Use small illustrative integers already in the chapter if any (e.g. '8K prompt', 3-4 sequences); invent NO absolute millisecond values on the axis (keep time relative/schematic). Keep lanes, arrows, and labels non-overlapping and entirely within the viewBox; fully understandable as a static frame — a playhead sweep would be optional enhancement only, not required.

summary: The chapter is already served by two strong existing figures — the disaggregated architecture dataflow (keep, with a small refine: pull the two edge-crowding badges inward and make animated pills start hidden so the static first frame is clean) and the chunked-prefill iteration timeline (keep as-is, excellent). Both, however, depict CONCLUSIONS and SOLUTIONS. The two genuinely foundational intuitions that nothing visualizes are (1) WHY the two phases have opposite bottlenecks — a weight-REUSE contrast (fat matrix x matrix prefill vs skinny vector x matrix decode, same HBM bytes, opposite arithmetic intensity), which is the physical bedrock of the whole chapter (priority 1); and (2) the interference PROBLEM itself — a gantt timeline where a long monolithic prefill stalls steady decode and blows out P99 ITL, the motivating 'before' that sets up both remedies (priority 2). Two new static figures, disciplined and non-duplicative.
